findings.replacement: SHA-256 / SHA-3-256 (or SHA-384+ for long-term assurance)
findings.notes: Collisions trivially producible. Unsafe for any security purpose. If used as a non-cryptographic content hash (cache keys, ETags, content addressing), changing to SHA-256 will alter output bytes — coordinate the change with downstream consumers.

findings.replacement: SHA-256 / SHA-3-256 (or SHA-384+ for long-term assurance)
findings.notes: Practical collisions demonstrated (SHAttered, 2017). Deprecated by NIST. If used as a non-cryptographic content hash (e.g. git object IDs, dedup keys), changing to SHA-256 will alter output bytes — coordinate with downstream consumers.

findings.replacement: SHA-256 / SHA-3-256 (or SHA-384+ for long-term assurance)
findings.notes: Practical collisions demonstrated (SHAttered, 2017). Deprecated by NIST. If used as a non-cryptographic content hash (e.g. git object IDs, dedup keys), changing to SHA-256 will alter output bytes — coordinate with downstream consumers.

findings.replacement: ML-KEM (FIPS 203) for key encapsulation; ML-DSA (FIPS 204) for signatures
findings.notes: Broken by Shor's algorithm on a CRQC. Migrate before long-lived secrets are at risk.
